sourceMovie.title: Heat
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Heat works as a crime movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Little Caesar
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Heat clicked for you, Little Caesar (1931) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Usual Suspects
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Heat clicked for you, The Usual Suspects (1995) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Shoot 'Em Up
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Heat clicked for you, Shoot 'Em Up (2007) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and action, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Heat?
faq.answer: Try Little Caesar, The Usual Suspects, Changeling—each shares DNA with Heat while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Heat?
faq.answer: Heat (1995) is best described as Crime, Drama, Action. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Heat part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Heat Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
